surveyNote: NEU online survey, 5–16 February 2026. 9,408 teacher members in English state schools.

editorial.standfirst: The NEU's State of Education: AI survey exposes a fault line in English state schools: 76% of teachers now use AI in their daily work, yet 49% of schools have no AI policy for staff or pupils. The findings — drawn from 9,408 NEU members surveyed in February 2026 — point to rapid staff adoption, deepening concerns over pupil learning, and institutional lag on governance.
editorial.methodology: Unless stated otherwise, figures are from the NEU release (February 2026). Training comparisons cite Sutton Trust polling (April 2025).

sections.title: Staff adoption gathers pace

teacher.prose:
Teacher reliance on AI has risen sharply — from 53% in 2025 to 76% today — driven chiefly by workload pressure rather than institutional strategy. Most colleagues are self-taught; adoption is outpacing training and policy by a wide margin.

The pattern of use is revealing. AI has become a default tool for resource creation and planning, while marking remains largely human work. One respondent warned that without guidance, staff risk "simply churning out sub-standard work" in the rush to save time.

leader.prose:
At 76% regular use, AI is already embedded in everyday school practice — with or without leadership sign-off. The question is no longer whether staff will use these tools, but whether the school has a shared framework for doing so safely.

Adoption is concentrated in resource creation (61%) and lesson planning (41%), with administrative tasks at 38%. Marking, at just 7%, suggests teachers still treat assessment as a professional judgement call — for now.

bars.l: Use AI regularly for day-to-day work

sections.note: Source: NEU survey of 9,408 teacher members in English state schools (February 2026).

quote.t: Teachers are clearly desperate for workload relief. But without proper guidance, some are simply churning out sub-standard work.

sections.title: Impact on pupil learning

teacher.prose:
66% of secondary teachers say pupils' critical thinking has declined because of AI use — against 28% in primary. The gap suggests the problem intensifies once pupils can access tools independently, often before they have learned to interrogate outputs.

NEU General Secretary Daniel Kebede argues that "students must be able to think for themselves" — yet the survey data shows reliance on AI is already affecting the skills assessments are meant to measure. Key Stage 3 may be the critical window for AI literacy and assessment redesign.

leader.prose:
If two thirds of secondary teachers report declining critical thinking, this is a whole-school pedagogical issue — not a problem for individual classrooms to manage alone. Primary colleagues report the trend less acutely (28%), but the direction of travel is clear as access widens.

Leaders should ask a direct question: do current assessments still measure what you intend in a world where pupils can reach for AI at any moment? If not, what are they actually testing?

split.l: Secondary teachers reporting a decline in pupils' critical thinking due to AI

sections.chartLbl: Teachers agreeing critical thinking has declined (due to AI use)

quote.t: Students must be able to think for themselves. This is at the heart of learning, but our survey shows a reliance on AI is having an effect on students' ability to think critically.

sections.title: Governance and training gaps

teacher.prose:
49% of schools have no AI policy for staff or pupils; 66% lack guidance specifically on pupil use. Teachers are making daily professional judgement calls without institutional cover — a safeguarding as well as a workload issue.

Separate Sutton Trust research underlines the training deficit: just 21% of state-school teachers had received formal AI training by April 2025, against 45% in independent schools. Policy absence and training inequality are two sides of the same governance failure.

leader.prose:
Nearly half of schools report no AI policy whatsoever — an urgent governance risk. In the absence of published guidance, staff and pupils improvise, and the school has no shared framework if something goes wrong.

The Sutton Trust gap (21% state vs 45% independent for formal training) sits alongside the NEU's policy findings. Both point to structural under-investment rather than individual reluctance.

Schools with no AI policy specifically for pupil use

bars.l: State-school teachers with formal AI training
Independent-school teachers with formal AI training

sections.sourceNote: Policy figures: NEU, February 2026. Training bars: Sutton Trust Artificial advantage? (Teacher Tapp polling, April 2025).

teacher.prose:
Only 14% of teachers support the Department for Education's plan to introduce AI tutors for disadvantaged pupils. 49% oppose it outright; 36% expressed no view when surveyed in February 2026, a week after the announcement.

Frontline concerns are consistent: AI cannot substitute for human empathy, professional judgement, or the relationships that make teaching work. Kebede has warned the government risks pupil development by deploying AI tutoring before long-term impacts are understood.

leader.prose:
Before adopting any externally mandated AI tool, consult staff and assess pupil impact. What does it replace? What does it risk? Who is accountable if it gives incorrect information?

With 14% support, 49% opposition and 36% undecided, the plan lacks a mandate from the profession. Opposition was strongest in special schools and pupil referral units — settings where the human relationship is often most critical.

quote.t: AI cannot be used as a cheap sticking plaster for underfunded schools or a replacement for real support staff and qualified teachers.

teacher.prose: The NEU is calling for structural change — curriculum time for AI literacy, properly funded training, and pupil-facing policies written with the profession. In the meantime, three practical steps matter in the classroom.

actions.title: Push for AI literacy in the curriculum
actions.body: Request dedicated time to teach pupils about AI limitations — hallucinations, bias, and when not to trust outputs. Frame it as safeguarding as well as digital skills.

actions.title: Co-design your school AI policy
actions.body: Do not wait for a policy to land from above. Ask for a seat at the table. Strong policies are written with teachers, parents, and pupils — not by leadership alone.

actions.title: Make the case for training funding
actions.body: Use NEU and Sutton Trust data to argue for sustained CPD — not a one-off INSET. Formal training gaps between state and independent schools are an equity issue as well as a professional one.

leader.prose: Three governance actions leaders and governors can take this term — drawn from the NEU framework and the survey data above.

actions.title: Commission an AI literacy audit
actions.body: Find out what pupils and staff already know about AI limitations. The results often surprise — and give you evidence to argue for curriculum time.

actions.title: Publish a pupil-facing AI policy this term
actions.body: Two thirds of schools still lack a pupil-specific policy. Yours can be among the minority that does. Involve teachers, pupils, and parents in drafting — not only senior leadership.

actions.title: Budget for sustained AI CPD
actions.body: One INSET afternoon will not close the training gap. Commission a programme with a named lead and clear outcomes.
